does: This paper presents a systematic evaluation of unsupervised inference-time scaling methods for reducing hallucinations in large language models without requiring trained verifiers or reward models. The authors construct a score × decoder grid pairing four intrinsic scoring functions (perplexity, contrastive, power-distribution likelihood, and self-verification) with three decoding families (optimization, sampling, and consensus) and evaluate all 12 combinations on MATH500 using Qwen3-1.7B models. The key finding is that self-verification enhanced with a training-free virtual-thinking prefix performs well across most settings, but no single score exhibits consistent quality independent of the decoder or model capability. The work demonstrates that effective hallucination mitigation requires careful joint selection of scoring function and decoding strategy rather than relying on any universally superior approach.
connects: This work extends the growing body of research on inference-time scaling and test-time scaling methods that have shown language models can surface latent knowledge through various decoding strategies. It builds on prior work comparing different verification approaches and relates to earlier studies on using model self-verification and consistency methods for improving answer reliability. The paper responds to limitations of supervised approaches like trained verifiers by systematically exploring what intrinsic signals within base models can identify correct outputs. It connects to broader research on prompt-based approaches like chain-of-thought and verification techniques, as well as classical decoding methods including sampling and consensus-based approaches. The exploration of multiple scoring signals draws on diverse techniques from the verification and scaling literature.
enables: This work opens directions for developing adaptive inference-time scaling systems that can select appropriate score-decoder combinations based on task and model characteristics without supervision. It suggests future research into understanding why certain score-decoder pairings work better for specific tasks and capability levels, potentially leading to principled guidelines for practitioners. The finding that virtual-thinking prefixes improve self-verification without training provides a scalable mechanism for enhancing unsupervised methods. The systematic framework could enable more efficient exploration of hybrid approaches combining multiple scores or decoders. Additionally, this establishes a foundation for investigating whether similar analysis applies to other domains beyond mathematics, and for developing methods that dynamically adapt their scoring and decoding strategies during inference based on output characteristics.
